Federal Reserve officials are widely expected to make their first interest rate cut in more than four years next week, and the fresh inflation reading — which was the coolest in more than three years — is the final major economic puzzle piece before that move. The report offered the latest evidence that inflation is coming under control after surging in 2021 and 2022. Given that, it should allow the Fed to turn a corner after two years of using higher interest rates to fight back against rapid price increases. Investors widely expect that Fed officials will cut interest rates at the conclusion of their policy meeting next Tuesday and Wednesday. They have been betting that officials will most likely lower borrowing costs by a quarter of a percentage point from their current 5.33 percent, but a larger half-point move is also seen as possible. Here’s what else to know: Underlying inflation trends: Stripped of food and fuel prices — which are volatile from month to month — the “core” inflation measure in August was 3.2 percent, in line with economist forecasts. Cool monthly numbers: Overall inflation was mild on a monthly basis, but the “core” measure was slightly quicker, ticking up to 0.3 percent, which was hotter than expected. Housing in focus: That uptick came as a closely watched housing measure , which tracks how much it would cost to rent owned housing, moved up. Economists are likely to keep a close eye on that, because it has been expected to slow. Stubbornness there could make it harder to fully stamp out inflation. The Fed’s calculus: Raising interest rates is like tapping the brakes on the economy. As economic activity slows thanks to higher borrowing costs, demand falls, making it more difficult for businesses to raise prices. Now, Fed officials want to make sure that they don’t overdo it. Keeping rates too high for too long could push up unemployment and risk a recession. Smaller September? The stubbornness in the monthly core inflation measure probably shores up the argument for a quarter-point rate cut in September, rather than a bigger half-point move. Investors were already betting that a smaller move was more likely. And then what? In the long run, what is likely to be even more consequential than how much the Fed cuts in September is how quickly they move in the months after that. We’ll get a snapshot of their plans next week, when they release fresh quarterly economic forecasts. Those will outline a rough path ahead for rates. Inflation continued to cool on an annual basis in August, good news for Federal Reserve officials ahead of their meeting next week, at which they are widely expected to make their first interest rate cut in more than four years. The politics of inflation: As price increases return to normal, consumers are beginning to feel like they are catching up. Wage growth has been faster than price increases for more than a year now by several measures. That is good news for the Biden administration, which has struggled to take credit for its economic wins — including a solid job market — as rising prices undermined consumer confidence. Jordyn Holman Despite inflation easing, many Americans continue to struggle. That is evident at certain retailers, such as the dollar store chains. Dollar General, whose core customer earns less than $35,000 a year, offered a telling detail on its earnings call: the last week of the month had been “the weakest by far” in recent months, suggesting that paychecks were running out. Ben Casselman Car insurance prices were up a whopping 16.5 percent from a year earlier in August, but that actually represents and improvement from July, when they were up 18.6 percent. That may signal that rapid increases in insurance rates may finally be easing, according to Jerry, a car insurance brokerage. Ben Casselman “The worst is behind us and we should continue to see more stability in rates going forward,” said Josh Damico, vice president of insurance operations for Jerry. “We’re hearing from carriers that they’ve stopped filing for new rate increases with state regulators and have pivoted toward looking for ways to increase new business with policy sales.” Advertisement Danielle Kaye So far, the reaction in the stock market is mixed, though fairly muted, as trading gets underway. The S&P 500 is down roughly 0.5 percent, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq is up about 0.1 percent. Investors are processing signs that a larger, half-point rate cut from the Fed is probably out of the picture. Data delayed at least 15 minutes Source: FactSet By The New York Times Jeanna Smialek Laura Rosner-Warburton, senior economist at MacroPolicy Perspectives, said this report probably argues for a quarter-point Fed rate cut this month. Jeanna Smialek “It suggests don’t go super fast here,” she said. “We’ve come a long way” but “we are not completely out of the woods.” Ben Casselman Falling energy prices helped bring down overall inflation in August, as did a decline in one of the highest-profile examples of pandemic inflation: used car prices. But car insurance and maintenance costs continued to rise rapidly. Airline fares rose sharply in August, but are still lower than they were a year ago. Lydia DePillis Lael Brainard, the White House’s National Economic Advisor, echoed a Harris debate line, saying in a statement that the nation was “turning the page on inflation.” Lydia DePillis She also alluded to a task that the Federal Reserve is starting to think more about: Preventing the job market from deteriorating further than it already has. “With inflation coming back down close to normal levels, it is important to focus on sustaining the historic gains we have made for American workers during this recovery,” she wrote. Lydia DePillis Finally, Brainard said that Republicans would “raise costs by nearly $4,000 per family,” a charge that likely comes from Trump’s proposals to raise tariffs on imports across the board. Economists have provided a range of estimates for how much that would pass through to American consumers, with $4,000 on the high end . Advertisement Ben Casselman Stop me if you’ve heard this before: Inflation is cooling, but housing costs remain stubborn. Overall consumer price growth continued to moderate in August, bringing inflation to a three-year low. But the “core” index, which excludes volatile food and energy prices, came in slightly hotter than expected. That was almost entirely because of shelter costs, which accounted for more than 70 percent of the year-over-year increase in core prices. Housing costs were up 5.2 percent in August from a year earlier, a slight uptick from July after several months of slow but steady declines in shelter inflation. The unexpected increase was primarily the result of costs for homeowners, which were up 5.4 percent from a year earlier. Housing has been a persistent thorn in the side of the Federal Reserve, even as prices in most other categories have moderated. Early this year, a surprise pickup in homeownership costs led to a brief re-acceleration in overall inflation, contributing to the Fed’s decision to hold off on interest rate cuts. Housing inflation subsequently began to cool again, but the data released on Wednesday suggested the process wouldn’t be smooth. Economists pay close attention to housing because it is by far the largest monthly expense for most families, and gets the most weight in the Consumer Price Index as a result. It will be hard for the Fed to get inflation all of the way back to its target of 2 percent annual price growth as long as housing costs keep rising significantly faster than they were before the pandemic. But inflation indexes take a somewhat confusing approach to measuring housing costs, especially for homeowners. Rather than looking at monthly expenses like mortgage payments, insurance and maintenance, government economists estimate what it would cost to rent out owner-occupied homes — an approach meant to account for the fact that buying a home is an investment, not just a consumer purchase. And its measure of rents comes at a significant lag. Private-sector data from firms like Zillow and Apartment List have shown rent increases slowing for roughly two years now. Forecasters expect the government’s measure of housing costs to follow — eventually. But that process has taken longer than most economists had expected. She has promised, for example, to prevent food companies and grocers from artificially inflating prices. We’ll get a snapshot of their plans next week, when they release fresh quarterly economic forecasts. Those will outline a rough path ahead for rates. Inflation continued to cool on an annual basis in August, good news for Federal Reserve officials ahead of their meeting next week, at which they are widely expected to make their first interest rate cut in more than four years. The politics of inflation: As price increases return to normal, consumers are beginning to feel like they are catching up. Wage growth has been faster than price increases for more than a year now by several measures. That is good news for the Biden administration, which has struggled to take credit for its economic wins — including a solid job market — as rising prices undermined consumer confidence. Jordyn Holman Despite inflation easing, many Americans continue to struggle. That is evident at certain retailers, such as the dollar store chains. Dollar General, whose core customer earns less than $35,000 a year, offered a telling detail on its earnings call: the last week of the month had been “the weakest by far” in recent months, suggesting that paychecks were running out. Ben Casselman Car insurance prices were up a whopping 16.5 percent from a year earlier in August, but that actually represents and improvement from July, when they were up 18.6 percent. That may signal that rapid increases in insurance rates may finally be easing, according to Jerry, a car insurance brokerage. Ben Casselman “The worst is behind us and we should continue to see more stability in rates going forward,” said Josh Damico, vice president of insurance operations for Jerry. “We’re hearing from carriers that they’ve stopped filing for new rate increases with state regulators and have pivoted toward looking for ways to increase new business with policy sales.” Advertisement Danielle Kaye So far, the reaction in the stock market is mixed, though fairly muted, as trading gets underway. The S&P 500 is down roughly 0.5 percent, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq is up about 0.1 percent. Investors are processing signs that a larger, half-point rate cut from the Fed is probably out of the picture. Data delayed at least 15 minutes Source: FactSet By The New York Times Jeanna Smialek Laura Rosner-Warburton, senior economist at MacroPolicy Perspectives, said this report probably argues for a quarter-point Fed rate cut this month. Jeanna Smialek “It suggests don’t go super fast here,” she said. “We’ve come a long way” but “we are not completely out of the woods.” Ben Casselman Falling energy prices helped bring down overall inflation in August, as did a decline in one of the highest-profile examples of pandemic inflation: used car prices. But car insurance and maintenance costs continued to rise rapidly. Airline fares rose sharply in August, but are still lower than they were a year ago. Lydia DePillis Lael Brainard, the White House’s National Economic Advisor, echoed a Harris debate line, saying in a statement that the nation was “turning the page on inflation.” Lydia DePillis She also alluded to a task that the Federal Reserve is starting to think more about: Preventing the job market from deteriorating further than it already has. “With inflation coming back down close to normal levels, it is important to focus on sustaining the historic gains we have made for American workers during this recovery,” she wrote. Lydia DePillis Finally, Brainard said that Republicans would “raise costs by nearly $4,000 per family,” a charge that likely comes from Trump’s proposals to raise tariffs on imports across the board. Economists have provided a range of estimates for how much that would pass through to American consumers, with $4,000 on the high end . Advertisement Ben Casselman Stop me if you’ve heard this before: Inflation is cooling, but housing costs remain stubborn. Overall consumer price growth continued to moderate in August, bringing inflation to a three-year low. But the “core” index, which excludes volatile food and energy prices, came in slightly hotter than expected. That was almost entirely because of shelter costs, which accounted for more than 70 percent of the year-over-year increase in core prices. Housing costs were up 5.2 percent in August from a year earlier, a slight uptick from July after several months of slow but steady declines in shelter inflation. The unexpected increase was primarily the result of costs for homeowners, which were up 5.4 percent from a year earlier. Housing has been a persistent thorn in the side of the Federal Reserve, even as prices in most other categories have moderated. Early this year, a surprise pickup in homeownership costs led to a brief re-acceleration in overall inflation, contributing to the Fed’s decision to hold off on interest rate cuts. Housing inflation subsequently began to cool again, but the data released on Wednesday suggested the process wouldn’t be smooth. Economists pay close attention to housing because it is by far the largest monthly expense for most families, and gets the most weight in the Consumer Price Index as a result. It will be hard for the Fed to get inflation all of the way back to its target of 2 percent annual price growth as long as housing costs keep rising significantly faster than they were before the pandemic. But inflation indexes take a somewhat confusing approach to measuring housing costs, especially for homeowners. Rather than looking at monthly expenses like mortgage payments, insurance and maintenance, government economists estimate what it would cost to rent out owner-occupied homes — an approach meant to account for the fact that buying a home is an investment, not just a consumer purchase. And its measure of rents comes at a significant lag. Private-sector data from firms like Zillow and Apartment List have shown rent increases slowing for roughly two years now. Forecasters expect the government’s measure of housing costs to follow — eventually. But that process has taken longer than most economists had expected. Lydia DePillis The Harris campaign often gets questions about how its proposal to help first-time homebuyers, providing $25,000 in down payment assistance, wouldn’t simply lead to even higher prices. Jared Bernstein, chair of the Council of Economic Advisors, said in an interview that “it’s really a question of sequencing” — once housing supply increases, home sellers won’t have the leverage to extract more money, he argued. Lydia DePillis The White House was ready for the role housing would play in this month’s report, and just published a blog post noting that the other components of "core" inflation have been subsiding steadily. Advertisement Lydia DePillis The latest data offers a mixed bag for Vice President Kamala Harris as she campaigns for the White House. The report is consistent with a picture of inflation that is cooling overall. But it will probably still fuel attacks from Republicans that the Biden-Harris administration has not brought prices under control more quickly. Housing was a primary driver of both the headline growth number and the core inflation rate. Rents have been slow to fall after spiking in recent years, and the cost of homeownership has also frustrated many younger Americans, a key voter demographic. Ms. Harris has homed in on that sentiment during speeches and in last night’s debate with former President Donald J. Trump. “We know that we have a shortage of homes and housing, and the cost of housing is too expensive for far too many people,” Ms. Harris said. She noted her proposal to give first-time home-buyers $25,000 in down payment assistance and incentivize home builders with the goal of producing three million new units. S ome economists have questioned whether that down payment assistance could actually exacerbate the run-up in housing prices given it is likely to fuel already strong demand. The Harris campaign has said that her plan to incentivize more home building will help increase the limited supply of housing. The White House emphasized policies it hopes to enact to further bring down inflation. “We’re still seeing pressure from housing costs, but we have aggressive plans to quickly boost the supply of affordable housing, plans we need Congress to work with us on ASAP,” the chair of the Council of Economic Advisers, Jared Bernstein, wrote in an email. Recent surveys have shown that even as cost growth has moderated and incomes have risen, Americans still think about current price levels as much worse than they remember just a few years ago. Recent data from the University of Michigan survey of consumers, for example, showed a rise in the share of respondents who identify high prices as a source of their financial difficulties. In the August survey by the National Federation of Independent Business, 24 percent of business owners said inflation was their top concern . The vice president has focused many of her other proposals squarely on either constraining price growth or helping Americans afford rising costs. She has promised, for example, to prevent food companies and grocers from artificially inflating prices. But it remains unclear exactly how such an anti-price-gouging policy would work, and people close to the planning have said it is unlikely to have much immediate impact on food prices. Lydia DePillis As the table is set for the Fed’s first rate cut of this cycle, it’s worth remembering that former President Trump’s primary proposal to contain housing costs — along with expelling unauthorized immigrants — is to bring down mortgage costs, a process that is already underway as markets anticipate lower Fed rates. Joe Rennison Bets on a larger-than-normal cut to rates next week have almost vanished from the market. Investors are now pricing in a typical quarter-point move when the central bank announces its rate decision a week from today. Danielle Kaye Investors hoping for a bigger rate cut might be disappointed by today’s report, said Chris Larkin, head of trading and investing at E-Trade. “But with inflation seemingly under control, the markets will likely turn their focus back to the economic growth side of the equation — especially the employment picture,” he said. Advertisement Jordyn Holman While easing inflation is good news for consumers, retail executives have said a Fed rate cut might be the deciding factor in people making large purchases again. Lowe’s chief executive Marvin Ellison said recently that consumers were still holding off on projects like kitchen remodels because of high rates. He did offer some hope, though: “Those projects rarely get canceled, they get delayed,” he said on an earnings call. Danielle Kaye U.S. government bond yields rose on the heels of the report, with both the 2-year and 10-year Treasury inching higher. It’s a sign of investors girding for rates to remain elevated for longer, since month-over-month “core” inflation didn’t slow by as much as expected. Joe Rennison “This isn’t the CPI report the market wanted to see,” said Seema Shah, chief global strategist at Principal Asset Management. Madeleine Ngo Food price gains continued to ease in August, providing some relief for consumers at the grocery store. Overall, food prices climbed 0.1 percent over the month, a slightly slower rate than July, when prices rose 0.2 percent. Grocery prices were flat after they rose 0.1 percent the month before. The cost of dining out rose 0.3 percent over the month, slightly up from 0.2 percent in July. Compared with a year earlier, food inflation also cooled. Food prices were up 2.1 percent in the year through August, down from 2.2 percent in July. The annual rate has, for the most part, been steadily easing since reaching a peak of 11.4 percent in August 2022. Prices for fruits and vegetables fell 0.2 percent over the month after they rose 0.8 percent in July. Meats, poultry and fish prices rose 0.5 percent after they climbed 0.3 percent the month before. Prices for cereals and bakery products fell 0.1 percent. Egg prices increased again after several months of relatively strong price gains. In August, egg prices rose 4.8 percent over the month, a slightly slower rate compared with July, when they climbed 5.5 percent. Egg prices have been up and down since an outbreak of bird flu contributed to a spike in prices early last year. Compared with a year ago, egg prices are up 28.1 percent. Although food inflation has eased, many consumers remain frustrated over high grocery costs, which has been a persistent issue for the Biden administration. The surge in food costs has been a top issue among voters ahead of the presidential election. Still, some consumers said they had noticed more moderate price increases at the grocery store in recent months. “I’m not seeing stuff go up as much,” said Nicole Jesse, 62, a co-owner of La Casa Pizzaria in Omaha, Neb. “I’m hopeful that maybe we won’t see prices continue to increase like they did.” Ms. Jesse said she had also experienced some relief with her business expenses as costs for ingredients like cheese and ground beef had started to stabilize over the past year. Still, she said she would most likely have to increase her restaurant’s menu prices by about 5 percent in the next few weeks to help offset higher labor, packaging and utility costs. Compared with a year earlier, the cost of dining out was up 4 percent in August, slightly down from 4.1 percent in July. “I’m really less concerned about overall food costs at this point,” Ms. Jesse said. “It’s more or less now just keeping up with things like wages.” Advertisement Jeanna Smialek The stubbornness in the monthly core inflation measure probably shores up the argument for a quarter point rate cut in September, rather than a bigger half-point move. Investors were already betting that a smaller move was more likely. Jeanna Smialek For the Fed, the tick up in monthly “core” inflation could be in focus here, especially because it came on the back of those stubborn housing costs. This doesn’t change the narrative — inflation is still cooling — but it is a cautionary note in the report. Lydia DePillis The moderate headline growth number is better for the Biden administration, particularly for Vice President Kamala Harris, than a reacceleration of inflation. But the stronger-than-expected core rate may provide ammunition for Republicans, especially given that it was primarily driven by stubborn housing costs. Jordyn Holman This report showed that food inflation continued to ease last month. That’s welcome news for consumers – and for the executives in the snack industry who have increasingly been noting that their customers are fed up with higher prices. The chief executive of Mondelez, the maker of Oreo and Ritz crackers, said on a recent earnings call that consumers “are frustrated with inflation, but they’re optimistic about the future.” Advertisement Ben Casselman “Core” inflation came in a bit hotter than expected, largely because of a pickup in housing costs. Housing has been a particularly stubborn inflation category, even as other prices have moderated. Joe Rennison Stocks nudged lower after the numbers were released and it seems this may be the reason why. Futures on the S&P 500 are down around 0.4 percent. Ben Casselman Housing costs accounted for more than 70 percent of the year-over-year increase in core prices. She also alluded to a task that the Fed is starting to think more about: Preventing the job market from deteriorating further than it already has. “With inflation coming back down close to normal levels, it is important to focus on sustaining the historic gains we have made for American workers during this recovery,” she wrote. Food inflation continued to ease in August. Overall, food prices climbed 0.1 percent over the month, slightly down from 0.2 percent in July. Grocery prices were flat over the month after they rose 0.1 percent in July. Ben Casselman The report is out! Consumer prices rose 0.2 percent in August and were up 2.5 percent from a year earlier. “Core” prices, excluding food and energy, were up 0.3 percent from July and 3.2 percent from a year ago. Ben Casselman The 2.5 percent year-over-year inflation reading is the lowest since March 2021. Lydia DePillis Prices still remains top of mind for American voters on the morning after a contentious debate , in which former President Donald Trump struggled to stick to his message tying Kamala Harris to rising costs. Lydia DePillis “I had no inflation, virtually no inflation, they had the highest inflation, perhaps in the history of our country because I’ve never seen a worse period of time,” Trump said. “People can’t go out and buy cereal bacon or eggs or anything else.” (Inflation was much higher in the early 1980s.) Advertisement Danielle Kaye With about 10 minutes until the report, stock futures on the S&P 500 and Nasdaq, which let investors buy and sell before the official start of trading, are slightly lower. The dip comes after the S&P 500 rose almost 0.5 percent yesterday. Futures for the Russell 2000, an index of smaller companies more closely tied to the ebb and flow of the economy, are also down a bit. Eshe Nelson On the other side of the Atlantic, tomorrow the European Central Bank is widely expected to announce another quarter-point interest rate cut, amid slowing inflation and weak economic growth in the eurozone. Eshe Nelson E.C.B. policymakers are still wary about services inflation, which accelerated last month, and have been taking a slow and cautious approach. Thursday’s expected cut would come three months after the first reduction in rates, and traders aren’t expecting another cut until December.

headline: Inflation cooled on a yearly basis in August.
body: U.S. inflation slowed to 2.5 percent in the year through August, but beneath the surface the details suggested that the costs of key categories, like housing, were stubborn. + 10 % Inflation + 8 + 6 +3.2% excluding food and energy + 4 +2.5% in Aug. + 2 0 – 2 ’05 ’10 ’15 ’20 + 14 % + 12 Inflation + 10 + 8 +3.2% excluding food and energy + 6 + 4 +2.5% in Aug. + 2 0 – 2 1965 ’70 ’75 ’80 ’85 ’90 ’95 2000 ’05 ’10 ’15 ’20 + 14 % + 12 Inflation + 10 + 8 + 6 +3.2% excluding food and energy + 4 +2.5% in Aug. + 2 0 – 2 1965 ’70 ’75 ’80 ’85 ’90 ’95 2000 ’05 ’10 ’15 ’20 Year-over-year change in the Consumer Price Index Source: Bureau of Labor Statistics By Karl Russell Jeanna Smialek The Consumer Price Index climbed 2.5 percent in August from a year earlier, a notably cooler pace of inflation than July’s 2.9 percent and down sharply from a peak of 9.1 percent back in 2022. Federal Reserve officials are widely expected to make their first interest rate cut in more than four years next week, and the fresh inflation reading — which was the coolest in more than three years — is the final major economic puzzle piece before that move. The report offered the latest evidence that inflation is coming under control after surging in 2021 and 2022. Given that, it should allow the Fed to turn a corner after two years of using higher interest rates to fight back against rapid price increases. Investors widely expect that Fed officials will cut interest rates at the conclusion of their policy meeting next Tuesday and Wednesday. They have been betting that officials will most likely lower borrowing costs by a quarter of a percentage point from their current 5.33 percent, but a larger half-point move is also seen as possible. Here’s what else to know: Underlying inflation trends: Stripped of food and fuel prices — which are volatile from month to month — the “core” inflation measure in August was 3.2 percent, in line with economist forecasts. Cool monthly numbers: Overall inflation was mild on a monthly basis, but the “core” measure was slightly quicker, ticking up to 0.3 percent, which was hotter than expected. Housing in focus: That uptick came as a closely watched housing measure , which tracks how much it would cost to rent owned housing, moved up. Economists are likely to keep a close eye on that, because it has been expected to slow. Stubbornness there could make it harder to fully stamp out inflation. The Fed’s calculus: Raising interest rates is like tapping the brakes on the economy. As economic activity slows thanks to higher borrowing costs, demand falls, making it more difficult for businesses to raise prices. Now, Fed officials want to make sure that they don’t overdo it. Keeping rates too high for too long could push up unemployment and risk a recession. Smaller September? The stubbornness in the monthly core inflation measure probably shores up the argument for a quarter-point rate cut in September, rather than a bigger half-point move. Investors were already betting that a smaller move was more likely. And then what? In the long run, what is likely to be even more consequential than how much the Fed cuts in September is how quickly they move in the months after that. We’ll get a snapshot of their plans next week, when they release fresh quarterly economic forecasts. Those will outline a rough path ahead for rates.
